coredata.dc:title: Energy levels of gapped graphene quantum dots in external fields
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bouhlal, Ahmed
El Azar, Mohammed
Siari, Ahmed
Jellal, Ahmed
coredata.dc:description:
We consider graphene quantum dots of radius R under the influence of a magnetic field B , an Aharonov–Bohm flux
Φ
A B
and an energy gap Δ . To study the associated energy levels, we first solve the eigenvalue equation in the two regions forming the present system to determine the eigenspinors for the valleys K and
K
′
. At the boundary R , we show that the continuity of the eigenspinors leads to an equation describing the energy levels as a function of the physical parameters
( B ,
Φ
A B
, Δ )
. As a first result, we show that the energy levels can be either symmetric or anti-symmetric with respect to the valley changes, depending on the values of the radius R . We observe that for small values of B , the energy levels exhibit degeneracy, which decreases with increasing B , coinciding with the convergence of the energy levels to the Landau levels. Furthermore, we show that the presence of
Φ
A B
leads to the creation of an energy gap. This gap extends the trapping time of the electrons without compromising the integrity of the system. On the contrary, the introduction of Δ widens the band gap and thus breaks the symmetry of the system by introducing new energy levels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Magnetic field
Quantum dot
Aharonov–Bohm flux
Energy levels
Energy gap
Landau levels

A B
and energy gap Δ .

K

.

head.ce:title: Energy levels of gapped graphene quantum dots in external fields

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaïb Doukkali University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We consider graphene quantum dots of radius R under the influence of a magnetic field B , an Aharonov–Bohm flux
Φ
A B
and an energy gap Δ . To study the associated energy levels, we first solve the eigenvalue equation in the two regions forming the present system to determine the eigenspinors for the valleys K and
K
′
. At the boundary R , we show that the continuity of the eigenspinors leads to an equation describing the energy levels as a function of the physical parameters
( B ,
Φ
A B
, Δ )
. As a first result, we show that the energy levels can be either symmetric or anti-symmetric with respect to the valley changes, depending on the values of the radius R . We observe that for small values of B , the energy levels exhibit degeneracy, which decreases with increasing B , coinciding with the convergence of the energy levels to the Landau levels. Furthermore, we show that the presence of
Φ
A B
leads to the creation of an energy gap. This gap extends the trapping time of the electrons without compromising the integrity of the system. On the contrary, the introduction of Δ widens the band gap and thus breaks the symmetry of the system by introducing new energy levels.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon-based electronics have become a promising technology due to the remarkable properties of graphene. Isolated in 2004, graphene is a single atomic layer of carbon arranged in a honeycomb lattice by
s
p
2
hybridization [1–3]. It possesses record-breaking properties such as high mobility and thermal conductivity [2,4], making it a leading candidate to replace silicon. Since then, the number of publications has grown exponentially, driven by promising theoretical predictions of exceptional electronic and mechanical properties. The distinctive band structure of graphene results from its hexagonal carbon lattice, where the valence and conduction bands meet at Dirac points K and
K
′
, called valleys [5]. Near these degenerate points, electrons behave as massless relativistic particles, obeying the Dirac equation even though they travel 300 times slower than light [6]. While graphene exhibits unique transport, its lack of bandgap precludes transistor applications requiring modulation [7]. Low signal and high leakage characterize the resulting devices [8]. However, progress is being made to overcome this limitation through band-structure engineering [9]. Future work may produce viable graphene transistors by inducing a band gap through substrates [10], dopants [11], or nanomesh patterning [12]. Ultimately, overcoming the band gap challenge could open new frontiers in digital logic and optoelectronics by exploiting the superior properties of graphene [2].

Developing a bandgap in graphene is critical for electronics. One approach is to cut graphene into nanoribbons, which theoretically induces a gap [13]. Alternatively, combining 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with 1D nanoribbons can reduce the dimensionality and introduce a gap through quantum confinement [14,15]. Isolating individual GQDs – often referred to as “artificial atoms” due to their atomic properties – yields tiny graphene fragments that exhibit quantum effects [14,15]. Significant Studies have been focused on trapping electrons in GQDs for a variety of applications, including quantum information [16], electronic circuits, gas detection [17], photovoltaic systems [18], and beyond. However, Klein tunneling challenges fermion confinement in graphene nanostructures, making device fabrication difficult [19]. Given this difficulty, scientists are actively investigating substitute techniques to suppress Klein tunneling. Consequently, a number of works have investigated the effect of magnetic confinement in GQDs [20–33].

The Aharonov–Bohm (AB) effect, first proposed theoretically by Yakir Aharonov and David Bohm in 1959, shows that electromagnetic fields can affect quantum particles even in regions where the fields are zero [34]. In the iconic AB experiment, an electron wave is split into two paths encircling either side of a long, thin solenoid carrying a magnetic flux [35]. Upon recombination, the electron wave exhibits an observable phase shift that depends on the magnetic flux through the solenoid loop, even though the electron wave does not enter the region of non-zero magnetic field [36]. This phenomenon is due to the vector potential, which depends on the topology of the electromagnetic field configuration rather than simply the local field strength [37]. Since its original proposal [34], the AB effect has been realized experimentally in a variety of systems [38–40] and has inspired topological concepts in modern physics [41]. It highlights the importance of global, geometrical properties of gauge fields and their ability to give rise to physically observable effects [42,43]. The AB phase is now regarded as the archetypal example of the connection between electromagnetic potentials and quantum interference [44]. The AB effect has been extensively studied in graphene systems through transport measurements on nanorings and antidot lattices. Some of the earliest observations were made by Russo et al. in 2008 [45], who fabricated monolayer graphene rings and detected AB conductance oscillations arising from constructive and destructive interference of electron waves circling the ring. The ability to robustly realize the AB effect in graphene opens new avenues for developing interferometry-based devices leveraging the material’s high mobility and tunability.

We study the energy levels of fermions confined in graphene sheets by a magnetic field to create GMQDs of radius R . These GMQDs are subjected to a mass term (energy gap) Δ and an Aharonov–Bohm (AB) flux
ϕ
i
. Our study relies on solving the Dirac equation to find eigenspinors in two regions separated by the radius R . Using the continuity of the eigenspinors at boundary, we derive an equation that describes the relation between the energy levels and the physical parameters that characterize our system. Due to the difficulty of obtaining an explicit solution for the energy, we opt for a numerical approach to highlight the fundamental features of our system. For this, we study the dependence of the energy levels on the quantum number m , the radius R , the magnetic field B , the energy gap Δ , and the AB flux
ϕ
i
. As a consequence, we demonstrate that the inclusion of
ϕ
i
expands the band gap, creating a separation between the valence and conduction bands without disrupting the symmetry of the energy levels. This extension enhances the trapping time of electrons within GMQDs. Additionally, the introduction of an energy gap also widens the band and allows new energy levels to appear, but it breaks the symmetry of energy. In conclusion, we assert that AB flux serves as a tunable parameter capable of controlling the electronic properties of GMQDs.

Optical spectroscopy methods are thought to be useful from an experimental perspective in clarifying the electronic spectrum of graphene magnetic quantum dots (GMQDs) [46–48]. In order to perform such analyses, it is essential to fabricate a graphene quantum dot sample and establish an appropriate magnetic field configuration. This requires that the sample be designed to facilitate optical access, which can be achieved either by deposition on a transparent substrate or by embedding in a matrix with optical properties conducive to spectroscopic studies. Optical spectroscopy requires the use of appropriate setups, such as photoluminescence (PL) or Raman spectroscopy, which require configuration with appropriate excitation sources, detectors, and spectral analysis capabilities. This setup should ensure precise control of the incident light and efficient collection of emitted or scattered light from the sample. GMQDs are excited by irradiation with photons of the required energy emitted by the excitation source. In PL spectroscopy, laser light illuminates the sample, while Raman spectroscopy uses monochromatic light to probe vibrational modes. Detectors integrated into the spectroscopy setup measure the light emitted or scattered by the sample. Spectra are recorded as a function of various parameters, including excitation energy and magnetic field strength, to provide comprehensive insight into the electronic spectrum of GMQDs. By following these procedures, optical spectroscopy can effectively unravel the electronic properties of GMQDs.

The present paper is structured as follows. The Hamiltonian is presented, and the spinors in the two regions are determined in Section 2. Using the boundary continuity conditions, we obtain an equation that gives the dependence of the energy levels on the parameters of GMQDs. In Section 3, the numerical results of our analysis are presented, and we give an interpretation of the results found. Finally, Section 4 presents the conclusions of this work.

To begin our study, we consider electrons in GMQDs of radius R generated by an applied magnetic field
B

which depends on parameters like energy

, magnetic field B , flux

, radius R , and quantum number m . It is noteworthy that solving Eq. (25) analytically is a challenging task. Therefore, resorting to a numerical solution becomes necessary. In this regard, we will present various numerical analyses of Eq. (25), exploring its fundamental characteristics to extract more pertinent information about GMQDs.

Fig. 2 illustrates the energy levels as a function of the magnetic field B for
R = 70
nm
, while varying the values of AB flux
ϕ
i
, energy gap Δ and quantum number m . For
ϕ
i
= 0
and
Δ = 0
, when a weak magnetic field tends towards zero (
B → 0
), we observe that the energy levels exhibit a continuous energy band as depicted in panels (a,b,c). This results in multiple degenerate states for all quantum number m and for both valleys K and
K
′
. At
B = 0
, there are a variety of degenerate zero-energy states for all values of m in both valleys K and
K
′
. However, as B increases, the number of degenerate zero-energy states for each m increases due to the breaking of the time-reversal symmetry. Furthermore, the degeneracy between the valleys K and
K
′
is suppressed due to the breaking of the inversion symmetry at the interface. For strong magnetic field, we note that the degeneracy is lifted while maintaining symmetry, allowing us to express
E
( m , s )
= E
( m , − s )
. This finding aligns with previous results [27,33,52,53]. Examining panels (d,e,f), the dependence of the energy levels on the magnetic field is evident with
R = 70
nm
and quantum number
m = − 1
(d),
m = 0
(e), and
m = 1
(f) for the two valleys: K illustrated by the blue dashed curves, and
K
′
shown by the magenta solid curves. These outcomes were obtained without introducing energy gap in the presence of the AB flux in GMQDs. A subtle separation of the energy levels, initially continuous at low magnetic fields, is observed, along with a notable opening of an energy gap between the conduction and valence bands. Analyzing panels (g,h,i), it becomes evident that a new energy level emerges at non-zero quantum number (
m ≠ 0
) when the magnetic field is less than 4 T (
B ≤ 4
T), leading to perturbations in the energy levels, as seen in panels (g,i). When the AB flux and energy gap are combined, the resulting band gap becomes larger, see panels (j,k,l). Notably, a new energy level emerges within the band gap for all values of quantum number m . The appearance of this new energy level is particularly conspicuous when
m = 1
, as observed in panel (l). According to our results, the presence of an energy gap leads to an increase in electron backscattering inside GMQDs while suppressing tunneling. This is consistent with the semiclassical understanding that a heavier particle is less likely to exhibit quantum effects such as tunneling. With the addition of the energy gap, which is responsible for the creation of new energy levels, the probability of electrons crossing from the valence band to the conduction band decreases. Importantly, this appearance is only observable when s is equal to one. The degeneracy of the energy levels is lost under the influence of a strong magnetic field B applied perpendicularly to the graphene sheet. As B increases, the energy levels of the charge carriers increasingly merge into GMQDs. At the center of GMQDs, the carriers become highly localized.

Fig. 3 shows the energy levels versus the radius R of GMQDs for a magnetic field
B = 10
T and three values of the quantum number
m = − 1 , 0 , 1
. Here, we vary the energy gap Δ and the AB flux
ϕ
i
. For
ϕ
i
= 0
and
Δ = 0
, see panels (a,b,c), an energy gap emerges between the electron and hole energy levels. As the radius R increases, this gap becomes smaller. We observe that when the value of R is very small, the energy levels for the two valleys K and
K
′
become nearly identical, indicating the symmetry
E
( m , s )
= E
( m , − s )
. In other cases, the system exhibits an anti-symmetry, which is
E
( m , s )
= − E
( m , − s )
. For a nonzero AB flux, we observe a significant increase in the band gap between the conduction and valence bands, leading to a better control of the electron flow, as shown in panels (d,e,f). As seen in panels (g,h,i), adding an energy gap
Δ = 50
meV creates new energy levels between the valence and conductance bands for all values of the quantum number
m = 0 , − 1 , 1
. These new energy levels exhibit an asymmetry in the energy levels, a result that is consistent with previous studies [27,33,52,53]. When both the energy gap and the AB flux are present, panels (j,k,l) show that the AB flux induces a widening of the band gap and a lowering of the energy levels between the conduction and valence bands. In addition, the symmetry of the energy levels appears to be broken.

Fig. 4 presents the energy levels E versus the energy gap Δ for
B = 10
T,
R = 70
nm
, and
m = 0 , − 1 , 1
, along with different values of the AB flux
ϕ
i
. When
ϕ
i
= 0
, the energy levels show changes with the energy gap, forming horizontal parabolas, confirming the symmetry
E
( m , s )
= E
( m , − s )
. Now, when the magnetic fields B and
ϕ
i
are present, the energy levels still show parabolic behavior, but differently. In fact, numerous additional energy states are introduced in this scenario, breaking the energy symmetries and widening the band gap. We conclude that the influence of the AB flux on the energy levels is particularly pronounced. This observation suggests that the energy gap is more efficiently regulated by both an externally applied magnetic field and a robust AB flux applied internally to GMQDs.

To present the energy levels as a function of the AB flux
ϕ
i
, we plot Fig. 5 for a strong magnetic field
B = 10
T,
R = 70
nm
,
m = 0 , − 1 , 1
, and
Δ = 0 , 50
meV. When
Δ = 0
, panels (a,b,c) show that the energy gaps between the valence and conduction bands are symmetric. In addition, the energy levels exhibit parabolic behavior with respect to the AB flux. For
Δ = 50
meV in panels (d,e,f) we observe that the existence of an energy gap breaks the symmetry and facilitates the emergence of new energy levels.

Let us consider the scenario where a strong magnetic field B is applied to GMQDs. Note that as B increases, the degenerate levels associated with each quantum number m undergo splitting, and the GMQDs states merge to form Landau levels. In particular, for strong B , the term
r
2
2
l
B
2
tends to zero, and therefore we can approximate the hypergeometric function by [54]
(26)
1
F
1
α , β , γ
=
Γ
( β )
Γ
( α )
e
γ
γ
α − β
1 + O
∣
γ
− 1
.
Utilizing the Gamma function to express binomial coefficients and applying the relation
Γ
( y + 1 )
= y Γ
( y )
, Eq. (25) can be simplified to
(27)
E
m n
= ±
ħ
v
F
l
B
2
n
2
+ 2
δ
2
+
| j |
+ j + 1 − s
where
n = 1 , 2 , 3 , …
is indexing the Landau levels, and
j = m − α +
ϕ
i
. Fig. 6 shows the energy
E
m n
(Eq. (27)) as a function of the quantum number m for
B = 10
T and
R = 70
nm
. For simplicity, we set
Δ = 0
and consider two values of the AB flux
ϕ
i
= 0 , 3 . 5
. We observe that
E
m n
is linear for negative values of m and becomes parabolic for positive values of m . For
ϕ
i
= 0
, the gap between the conduction and valence bands is zero in the valley K , but 229.4 meV in the valley
K
′
. For
ϕ
i
= 3 . 5
, the gap becomes non-zero and measures 429.2 meV for valley K and 486.7 meV for valley
K
′
, exceeding the previous value of 229.4 meV. It is evident that the presence of the AB flux either creates a band gap or increases the size of the band gap. A crucial observation is the asymmetry of
E
m n
with respect to the quantum number m and the valley s , indicating that
E
m n
( m , s )
≠
E
m n
( − m , s )
and
E
m n
( m , s )
≠
E
m n
( m , − s )
.

In Fig. 7, the radial probability
ρ
m
( ρ )
is plotted against the normalized radius
ρ =
r
R
of GMQDs for
B = 10
T, energy
E = 100
meV,
m = 0 , − 1 , 1
, and three values of the AB flux
ϕ
i
. For
ϕ
i
= 0
, the radial probability
ρ
m
( ρ )
is observed to be maximum and equal to one at the center (
ρ = 0
) of GMQDs when
s ⋅ m > 0
. This happens when
s = 1
and
m = 0 , 1
or when
s = − 1
and
m = 0 , − 1
. Now if
ϕ
i
≠ 0
, we notice that
ρ
m
( ρ )
is canceled at the center (
ρ = 0
) of GMQDs. The highest radial probability indicates that the electron is tightly confined in GMQDs. It is worth noting that the inclusion of the AB flux reduces the value of this maximum by shifting it towards the edge of GMQDs. It is important to note that the radial probability shows oscillatory behavior after the first maximum. In the presence of
ϕ
i
, there can be more than one state of electron confinement in GMQDs, leading to multiple trapped electrons. Consider a specific scenario where
ϕ
i
= 3 . 5
for
m = − 1
and
s = 1
, which corresponds to the K valley shown in Fig. 7(a), represented by the green line. As we can see, there are three peaks in the graph corresponding to
ρ = 0 . 26 , 0 . 62
, and 0.93. These values indicate that there are three different states of an electron that can be trapped inside GMQDs.

Comparing the results of this study with our previous work [55,56], several observations emerge from this study. When electrons are confined to regions on the order of their wavelength, such as in quantum dots or rings, external physical parameters become influential on the confinement. The Aharonov–Bohm flux, the electrostatic potential, and the energy gap are among the tunable factors that affect electron behavior. The intrinsic properties of our systems also play a key role. Graphene nanostructures exhibit electronic properties that depend on size, deformation, magnetic fields, etc. In particular, the Aharonov–Bohm flux provides tunable control over transport properties. The interplay between intrinsic system properties and external tuning parameters highlights the rich and adaptable nature of electron dynamics in quantum nanostructures. This cross-influence provides valuable insight into how electronic transports vary with confinement conditions. Overall, the nanostructuring of graphene enables highly modifiable confinement effects on electrons.

Our study focused on the magnetic field B confinement of Dirac fermions in GMQDs of radius R , where an AB flux
ϕ
i
and an energy gap Δ are applied. As a first step, we solved the Dirac equation based on the asymptotic behavior to obtain the eigenspinors for the valleys K and
K
′
. We then matched these solutions at the boundary of GMQDs to derive an equation describing the corresponding energy levels as a function of the physical parameters B , R ,
ϕ
i
and Δ .

In our numerical analysis, we focused on investigating the effect of the AB flux on the energy levels and radial probability of the trapped electrons. Indeed, for the energy levels as a function of the magnetic field B , we observed that at low values of B (
B → 0
), the energy levels exhibited degeneracy, but as B increased, they became more distinct. In particular, the symmetry
E
( m , s )
= E
( m , − s )
persisted even in the presence of the AB flux
ϕ
i
. This resulted in a widening of the energy gap, creating a separation between the valence and conduction bands and introducing new energy states. The presence the AB flux alone resulted in a wider band gap, but no new energy levels appeared. We then analyzed how the energy spectrum varied with radius R . Our analysis showed that the band gap decreased with increasing R . This conclusion held true when the effect of the AB flux on the energy levels was considered. Interestingly, the addition of the AB flux
ϕ
i
, led to an increase in the band gap without affecting the symmetry of the system or introducing new energy levels within the energy gap Δ .

Furthermore, we have shown that a strong magnetic field B causes the energy levels to converge to the Landau levels of graphene. This convergence indicates that both B and the Aharonov–Bohm flux
ϕ
i
decrease the energy levels. Remarkably, increasing
ϕ
i
significantly widens the energy gap, even in the absence of a mass term, which would break the symmetry in MGQDs. The flux
ϕ
i
enhances the fermion confinement by increasing the energy gap separation. Thus,
ϕ
i
emerges as a key tunable parameter to control the energy gap and modulate electron transport. It prolongs the trapping time of the electrons in GMQDs while allowing the manipulation of the energy levels. Note that analysis of the radial probability distribution reveals how electrons are spatially distributed under different confinement conditions. We have shown that the highest radial probability indicates tight confinement of the electrons in GMQDs.

The analysis of the radial probability provided insight into the bound state positions and spatial configurations of localized electrons and holes. Overall, the interplay between external tuning forces such as B and
ϕ
i
highlights the rich tunability of quantum confinement effects in graphene nanostructures.

We believe that our method for computing energy levels as a function of confinement radius, Aharonov–Bohm flux, and external magnetic field has broad applicability to other systems characterized by Dirac cones in electronic dispersion. Our approach is based solely on the solution of the Dirac equation, a fundamental low-energy model applicable to a wide range of two-dimensional materials, topological insulators, and artificial honeycomb structures. This can adequately capture the essential effects of confinement, provided that the quasiparticles of the system behave like massless Dirac fermions near the Fermi level. It is only necessary to consider different gauge fields and boundary conditions.

While our present study uses an analytical Dirac model, it would be valuable to validate these results using first-principles electronic structure methods such as density functional theory (DFT) calculations. DFT provides an ab initio treatment of the system without relying on the Dirac approximation. A comparison of DFT energy spectra with our predicted Dirac spectra over different fields and radii would serve as a crucial validation of the underlying assumptions and accuracy of the effective Dirac model. Any discrepancies between the two approaches may also provide new insights beyond the continuum picture. We intend to perform such DFT studies in future research to confirm the trends observed in this investigation from an independent computational perspective.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
